tourism_samples.title: Galle Fort
tourism_samples.description: Galle Fort, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is a historic coastal fortress with cobblestone streets, colonial buildings, museums, and cultural events.

tourism_samples.title: Unawatuna Beach
tourism_samples.description: Unawatuna is a famous beach near Galle with calm turquoise waters, snorkeling spots, and beachside cafes.

tourism_samples.title: Hikkaduwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Hikkaduwa is a lively beach town known for surfing, coral reefs, nightlife, and seafood restaurants.

tourism_samples.title: Mirissa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Mirissa is a southern coastal town with golden beaches, turquoise waters, whale watching, surfing, and local seafood restaurants.

tourism_samples.title: Bentota Beach
tourism_samples.description: Bentota is a popular beach resort town with water sports, mangrove boat rides, and luxury hotels.

tourism_samples.title: Sigiriya Rock Fortress
tourism_samples.description: Sigiriya, known as Lion Rock, is an ancient fortress with frescoes, water gardens, and terraced landscapes. Visitors can climb to the summit for panoramic views.

tourism_samples.title: Dambulla Cave Temple
tourism_samples.description: Dambulla is a UNESCO-listed cave temple complex with ancient Buddha statues and murals carved into rock.

tourism_samples.title: Anuradhapura Sacred City
tourism_samples.description: Anuradhapura is an ancient city with massive stupas, monasteries, and sacred Buddhist heritage sites.

tourism_samples.title: Temple of the Tooth Relic
tourism_samples.description: Located in Kandy, this sacred temple houses the tooth relic of Buddha and hosts daily religious rituals.

tourism_samples.title: Kandy Lake
tourism_samples.description: Kandy Lake is a scenic man-made lake in the heart of Kandy, perfect for evening walks and views of the Temple of the Tooth.

tourism_samples.title: Ella
tourism_samples.description: Ella is a tranquil hill town with lush tea plantations, waterfalls, and scenic hiking trails like Ella Rock and Little Adam’s Peak.

tourism_samples.title: Nine Arches Bridge
tourism_samples.description: The Nine Arches Bridge in Ella is a famous colonial-era railway bridge surrounded by jungle and tea fields.

tourism_samples.title: Horton Plains National Park
tourism_samples.description: Horton Plains is a highland plateau with cloud forests, grasslands, waterfalls, and the dramatic World’s End cliff.

tourism_samples.title: Nuwara Eliya
tourism_samples.description: Nuwara Eliya, known as Little England, is a cool hill town with tea plantations, lakes, waterfalls, and colonial architecture.

tourism_samples.title: Yala National Park
tourism_samples.description: Yala National Park is a wildlife sanctuary famous for leopards, elephants, and safari jeep tours.

tourism_samples.title: Udawalawe National Park
tourism_samples.description: Udawalawe is a popular wildlife park known for elephant sightings and wide open grasslands.

tourism_samples.title: Trincomalee
tourism_samples.description: Trincomalee is a coastal city with pristine beaches, Koneswaram Temple, diving spots, and historic forts.

tourism_samples.title: Nilaveli Beach
tourism_samples.description: Nilaveli is a beautiful white-sand beach near Trincomalee, ideal for snorkeling and relaxation.

tourism_samples.title: Arugam Bay
tourism_samples.description: Arugam Bay is a world-renowned surfing destination on the east coast, known for its laid-back vibe, point breaks, and vibrant nightlife.

tourism_samples.title: Polonnaruwa Ancient City
tourism_samples.description: The second ancient capital of Sri Lanka, featuring well-preserved ruins of royal palaces, temples, and the magnificent Gal Vihara rock sculptures.

tourism_samples.title: Galle Fort Dutch Museum
tourism_samples.description: The Dutch Period Museum inside Galle Fort showcases colonial artifacts, furniture, and history of Dutch rule in Sri Lanka.

tourism_samples.title: Galle Lighthouse
tourism_samples.description: The Galle Lighthouse is the oldest lighthouse in Sri Lanka, located within the Galle Fort walls with panoramic ocean views.

tourism_samples.title: Jungle Beach
tourism_samples.description: Jungle Beach is a secluded cove near Unawatuna surrounded by jungle, ideal for snorkeling and escaping the crowds.

tourism_samples.title: Japanese Peace Pagoda
tourism_samples.description: A serene Buddhist stupa on a hilltop near Unawatuna with stunning views over the Indian Ocean and surrounding coastline.

tourism_samples.title: Koggala Lake
tourism_samples.description: Koggala Lake near Galle is a scenic lagoon perfect for boat rides, birdwatching, and visiting small island temples.

tourism_samples.title: Rumassala Hill
tourism_samples.description: Rumassala is a forested hill near Unawatuna with jungle trails, medicinal herbs, and panoramic views of Galle Bay.

tourism_samples.title: Hikkaduwa Coral Reef
tourism_samples.description: The Hikkaduwa coral reef is a protected marine sanctuary near Galle with vibrant coral gardens and sea turtles.

tourism_samples.title: Kataragama Sacred City
tourism_samples.description: Kataragama is a multi-religious sacred town in southern Sri Lanka, home to the famous Kataragama Devalaya shrine, Buddhist dagobas, and the Menik Ganga river used for ritual bathing. It is revered by Buddhists, Hindus, Muslims, and indigenous Veddas.

tourism_samples.title: Pinnawala Elephant Orphanage
tourism_samples.description: Pinnawala Elephant Orphanage is a world-famous sanctuary that rescues and rehabilitates orphaned wild elephants. Visitors can watch elephants bathing in the Maha Oya river and interact with the largest captive elephant herd in the world.

tourism_samples.title: Ambuluwawa Tower
tourism_samples.description: Ambuluwawa Tower is a unique spiral biodiversity complex and tower located in Gampola, offering panoramic views of four provinces. The site features a multi-religious center, forest trails, and diverse flora and fauna.

tourism_samples.title: Knuckles Mountain Range
tourism_samples.description: The Knuckles Mountain Range is a UNESCO World Heritage listed mountain range in central Sri Lanka, known for its dramatic peaks, cloud forests, waterfalls, diverse wildlife, and challenging trekking trails.

tourism_samples.title: Jaffna Fort
tourism_samples.description: A historic fortress in the northern tip of Sri Lanka, reflecting both Portuguese and Dutch architectural influences and offering views of the Jaffna lagoon.

tourism_samples.title: Pidurangala Rock
tourism_samples.description: Pidurangala is a massive rock formation situated adjacent to Sigiriya Rock, offering an adventurous hike and the best panoramic sunset views of Sigiriya Lion Rock.

tourism_samples.title: Ritigala Archaeological Monastery
tourism_samples.description: Ritigala is an ancient Buddhist monastery and mountain range strict nature reserve containing beautiful stone ruins, bridges, and unique medicinal herbs.

tourism_samples.title: Delft Island
tourism_samples.description: Also known as Neduntheevu, Delft Island is a remote island off the Jaffna coast famous for its wild horses, giant baobab tree, growing coral walls, and historical Dutch ruins.

tourism_samples.title: Lipton's Seat
tourism_samples.description: Lipton's Seat is a famous panoramic viewpoint in Haputale where Sir Thomas Lipton used to survey his vast tea plantations, offering breathtaking views over multiple provinces.

tourism_samples.title: Diyaluma Falls
tourism_samples.description: Diyaluma Falls is the second-highest waterfall in Sri Lanka, popular for its natural swimming pools and infinity drop pools situated at the very top of the falls.

tourism_samples.title: Lunuganga Estate
tourism_samples.description: The country estate of the world-famous tropical modernist architect Geoffrey Bawa, displaying beautiful Italian-style gardens, lake views, and artistic landscaping.

tourism_samples.title: Pigeon Island National Park
tourism_samples.description: A beautiful marine national park in Nilaveli consisting of pristine coral reefs, sea turtles, colorful reef fish, and harmless blacktip reef sharks ideal for snorkeling.

tourism_samples.title: Sinharaja Forest Reserve
tourism_samples.description: Sinharaja is a UNESCO World Heritage biosphere reserve and primary tropical rainforest in Sri Lanka, boasting immense biodiversity and rare endemic birds and tree species.

tourism_samples.title: Bambarakanda Falls
tourism_samples.description: Bambarakanda is the tallest waterfall in Sri Lanka, cascading down a sheer rock face amidst beautiful pine forests.

tourism_samples.title: Adam's Peak
tourism_samples.description: Known locally as Sri Pada, Adam's Peak is a sacred mountain revered by multiple religions, famous for midnight pilgrimage hikes to watch the magnificent sunrise and its triangular shadow.

tourism_samples.title: Gal Oya National Park
tourism_samples.description: A remote wildlife sanctuary famous for its boat safaris on the Senanayake Samudra reservoir, where visitors can watch wild elephants swimming between islands.

tourism_samples.title: Belilena Cave
tourism_samples.description: An archaeological cave site near Kitulgala that holds evidence of the prehistoric Balangoda Man, who lived there over 12,000 years ago.

tourism_samples.title: Mannar Island
tourism_samples.description: A northern peninsula containing ancient baobab trees, historic Mannar Fort, migrating flamingos, and the sandbanks of Adam's Bridge stretching towards India.

tourism_samples.title: Nilgala Forest Reserve
tourism_samples.description: A medicinal forest reserve renowned for its savannah-like grasslands, wild herds of deer, rich medicinal herbs, and avian biodiversity.

tourism_samples.title: Dunhinda Falls
tourism_samples.description: One of Sri Lanka's most beautiful roaring waterfalls, located near Badulla and surrounded by lush green valleys.

tourism_samples.title: Sacred Unawatuna Viewpoint Peak

tourism_samples.title: Sacred Hikkaduwa Viewpoint Peak

tourism_samples.title: Ambalangoda Beach
tourism_samples.description: Ambalangoda is a coastal town roughly 30 km north of Galle, world-famous as Sri Lanka's centre of traditional devil-dance mask carving—the Ariyapala and Sons Mask Museum, operating since 1942, documents the craft in exhaustive detail. The town beach itself is a working fishing harbour with colourful trawlers and a briny, photogenic character rather than a swimming beach, while the coastline stretching south toward Balapitiya is calmer and sandier. The beach is accessible via the scenic Colombo–Matara coastal railway line, whose tracks run directly beside the shoreline here.

tourism_samples.title: Dodanduwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Dodanduwa is a quiet beachfront village between Hikkaduwa and Ambalangoda, distinguished by the Pushparama Maha Vihara Buddhist temple whose dagoba grounds extend almost to the water's edge—an unusual and photogenic juxtaposition of sacred architecture and sea. A small lagoon behind the beach contains a traditional yathra dhoni boat-building yard where the distinctive lateen-rigged fishing vessels of the south coast have been constructed for generations. The beach offers calm swimming in the northeast monsoon season and is popular as a local weekend picnic destination.

tourism_samples.title: Hikkaduwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Hikkaduwa is a 3 km strip of beach north of Galle, historically Sri Lanka's most internationally recognised tourist beach and surfing destination since the 1970s backpacker era. The northern end offers consistent reef and beach-break surf while the southern stretch fronts Hikkaduwa National Park's coral reef, where sea turtles, parrotfish, and reef sharks can be observed by snorkellers without hiring a boat. The town retains a lively strip of bars, restaurants, and surf-hire shops, making it the undisputed social hub of the budget-travel scene on the south coast.

tourism_samples.title: Hikkaduwa Coral Reef National Park
tourism_samples.description: Designated as a marine national park in 1979, the Hikkaduwa Coral Reef extends roughly one kilometre offshore and supports over 100 species of reef fish alongside a resident population of green and hawksbill sea turtles that approach snorkellers at very close range. Glass-bottomed boats and snorkel-hire operators line the beach directly in front of the reef, making it the most accessible coral ecosystem for non-divers anywhere in Sri Lanka. The park was damaged by coral bleaching in 1998 and by the 2004 tsunami, but active recovery is now well established.

tourism_samples.title: Balapitiya Beach
tourism_samples.description: Balapitiya sits at the mouth of the Madu Ganga, a Ramsar-protected mangrove river system sheltering 64 islets and 303 documented plant species, making it one of the most ecologically rich estuaries on the south coast. Boat tours depart from the beach landing to navigate mangrove tunnels and visit the Koth Duwa Island temple, where sacred golden carp crowd the stone steps. The beach itself is a broad sandy strand where traditional outrigger fishing canoes are pulled up under shade palms each afternoon.

tourism_samples.title: Kosgoda Turtle Hatchery Beach
tourism_samples.description: Kosgoda hosts one of the oldest and largest sea turtle conservation hatcheries in Sri Lanka, established in 1981 and having released well over a million hatchlings into the ocean since its founding. All five species that nest in Sri Lanka—green, hawksbill, leatherback, loggerhead, and olive ridley—have been recorded at Kosgoda, with nesting females coming ashore throughout the year and peak activity from September to April. Visitors can observe hatchlings in holding pools and release them at the water's edge at dusk under staff supervision.

tourism_samples.title: Peraliya Tsunami Memorial Coast
tourism_samples.description: The beach at Peraliya, immediately north of Hikkaduwa, is the site of the deadliest single train disaster in recorded history—on 26 December 2004 the Indian Ocean tsunami struck the Samudra Devi express, overturning all eight carriages and killing an estimated 1,700 passengers. A preserved train carriage and the Japanese-funded Tsunami Honganji Memorial Vihara stand above the beach as permanent memorials, and the shoreline's profile was permanently altered by the wave. The site forms part of the 6 km beach walk between Hikkaduwa and Ambalangoda.

tourism_samples.title: Habaraduwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Habaraduwa is a calm, shallow beach community east of Galle, popular with Sri Lankan families and day-trippers for its gentle, protected waters and the absence of the hustle found at more tourist-heavy stretches to the north. The shoreline is backed by casuarina trees and small fishing settlements, and at low tide the rock flats to the east expose rich tidal pools. The beach is directly accessible by the Colombo–Matara coastal railway, whose scenic tracks hug the coast through this section.

tourism_samples.title: Talpe Beach
tourism_samples.description: Talpe is a serene, rock-edged beach just east of Galle Fort, known for its calm tidal rock pools, traditional outrigger canoes, and a scattering of boutique guesthouses tucked behind the casuarina treeline. The water clarity at low tide reveals sea-grass beds that attract feeding hawksbill turtles, and the beach is wider and considerably less crowded than adjacent Unawatuna. Its location within easy cycling distance of Galle's heritage streets makes it popular with longer-stay visitors.

tourism_samples.title: Wijaya Beach, Talpe
tourism_samples.description: Wijaya Beach is a boutique coastal strip near Talpe village, fronted by a broad sand and rock flat at low tide that reveals tidal pools and dense sea-grass—ideal for kayaking and stand-up paddleboarding. The beach has attracted a cluster of high-quality guesthouses and villa properties developed for the luxury eco-tourism market, offering privacy rare on the more developed south coast. Its position directly on the coastal railway line makes it surprisingly accessible despite its quiet character.

tourism_samples.title: Koggala Beach
tourism_samples.description: Koggala is a long, open beach east of Galle, historically significant as the site of a Royal Air Force Catalina flying-boat base established in 1942 whose concrete slipways and control tower ruins are still visible near the adjacent lagoon. Koggala Lake, immediately behind the beach, is dotted with small islands including one hosting the Kataluwa Purvarama Buddhist temple, accessible by dugout canoe from the shore. The Martin Wickramasinghe Folk Museum, dedicated to Sri Lanka's greatest Sinhala novelist who was born in Koggala village, stands a few hundred metres inland.

tourism_samples.title: Galle Fort
tourism_samples.description: Galle Fort is a UNESCO World Heritage Site occupying the southwestern tip of Galle town, originally constructed by the Portuguese in 1588 and massively extended by the Dutch East India Company after 1649, its 36-hectare walled enclosure preserving colonial streets, a Dutch Reformed church, mosques, a lighthouse, and hundreds of occupied residences within rampart walls over three metres thick. Today it functions as a living heritage town with boutique hotels, jewellery shops, and art galleries operating from centuries-old Dutch warehouses, and the rampart walk at sunset is one of the south coast's defining experiences. The fort was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988 for its outstanding example of an urban ensemble illustrating the interaction of European architecture and South Asian traditions.

tourism_samples.title: Galle Lighthouse
tourism_samples.description: The Galle Lighthouse stands within the ramparts of Galle Fort at the southwestern tip of the peninsula, its white-painted tower rising 26 metres and serving as an active navigational aid since a first structure was erected by the British in 1848—the current octagonal tower dates from 1939. The lighthouse shares its narrow headland with the New Oriental Hotel, claimed to be the oldest hotel in Sri Lanka in continuous operation, originally built as the Dutch Governor's official residence. Sunset from the adjacent rampart walk, with the lighthouse silhouetted against an orange Indian Ocean sky, is one of the most reproduced images on the south coast.

tourism_samples.title: Rumassala Jungle Beach
tourism_samples.description: Rumassala is a thickly forested hill south of Unawatuna that shelters a small, largely undeveloped beach on its seaward flank, accessible only by a jungle trail or by boat. The hill is linked in Sri Lankan Ramayana tradition to Hanuman, the legend holding that he dropped a fragment of the Himalayas here while carrying medicinal herbs—a claim supported by the unusually high diversity of medicinal plant species recorded in the forest. The beach below offers good snorkelling on a reef protected by the headland's aspect, though access near the Navy establishment on the hilltop requires care.

tourism_samples.title: Unawatuna Beach
tourism_samples.description: Unawatuna is a sheltered crescent-shaped bay roughly 3 km south of Galle, flanked by tropical forest and the Rumassala headland, with calm turquoise waters ideal for swimming and snorkelling over patchy fringing reef. The bay hosts dozens of guesthouses, dive schools, and seafood restaurants along its 800-metre arc of sand, and is one of the most intensely developed beach destinations on the south coast. A Japanese Buddhist Peace Pagoda built by Nipponzan-Myohoji monks crowns the hill above the bay, offering panoramic views of the Indian Ocean.

tourism_samples.title: Ahangama Beach
tourism_samples.description: Ahangama is a quiet coastal village stretching between Koggala and Midigama, popular with professional and semi-professional surfers who return each season for its reliable reef breaks—including the performance wave at Kabalana Point. The shoreline between Ahangama and Weligama provides the most authentic setting to observe stilt fishermen balancing on cross-poles driven into the sea floor, a centuries-old fishing technique now partially performed for tourists. Several respected surf camps and yoga retreats operate from the network of lanes behind the beach.

tourism_samples.title: Kabalana Surf Point, Ahangama
tourism_samples.description: Kabalana Point is a powerful reef break at the western end of Ahangama, generating a hollow, fast left-hand wave over shallow reef on larger southwest swells during the May–October surfing season. The take-off demands intermediate to advanced experience, though a gentler reform section further along the reef is used for progression coaching by the surf schools operating from Ahangama village. The wave breaks directly in front of the coastal railway, and the sight of a southbound train passing while surfers ride the reef has become an iconic visual of the south coast.

tourism_samples.title: Midigama Beach
tourism_samples.description: Midigama is a small fishing and longboarding village between Ahangama and Weligama, home to two of Sri Lanka's most celebrated surf breaks—Lazy Left and the faster right-hander called Rams—both of which have been featured in international surf publications for decades. The village retains an exceptionally low-key character with a handful of small guesthouses and family-run surf camps amid coconut groves, and the pace of life remains primarily tied to fishing. The rocky headland at the eastern end of the beach provides a flat-water channel for paddling out to the reef breaks.

tourism_samples.title: Lazy Left Surf Break, Midigama
tourism_samples.description: Lazy Left is a world-renowned left-hand point break at Midigama, peeling slowly over a flat reef shelf and producing long, makeable waves perfectly suited to longboarding—it has been cited in international surf media as one of the finest longboard waves in Asia. The wave breaks best on a southwest swell of 1–2 metres and can produce rides of up to 200 metres with multiple nose-riding and cross-stepping sections. Its sensitivity to swell direction and size keeps line-ups manageable on many days, preserving a relaxed atmosphere even during peak season.

tourism_samples.title: Weligama Bay
tourism_samples.description: Weligama's wide, sweeping bay is Sri Lanka's most popular learn-to-surf destination, with a gentle beach break, almost no hazards, and numerous surf schools offering two-hour beginner lessons at competitive prices. The bay is also associated with the iconic image of stilt fishermen—men balanced on wooden poles planted in the shallows—a centuries-old technique now partly performed for tourists along the road between Weligama and Ahangama. Taprobane Island, a tiny private estate offshore crowned by a hilltop villa once owned by French author Count de Mauny-Talvande, is visible from the western end of the beach.

tourism_samples.title: Madiha Beach
tourism_samples.description: Madiha is a narrow beach just west of Mirissa, linked to the famous whale-watching harbour but separated from Mirissa's busier nightlife and surf scene by a low headland. Several large boulders divide the shoreline into intimate sections, and a steep shore break means the beach is better suited to sunbathing and rock-pool exploration than swimming. A handful of eco-friendly guesthouses operate from the village behind the beach, drawing travellers who want proximity to Mirissa's facilities without the associated noise.

tourism_samples.title: Mirissa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Mirissa is an internationally celebrated crescent bay on Sri Lanka's south coast, ranked among the world's premier locations for observing blue whales and sperm whales, with licensed whale-watching vessels departing from the harbour from November to April. The eastern end of the beach is anchored by Parrot Rock—a granite islet accessible at low tide—while gentle southwest surf rolls in providing beginner surfing conditions. Despite heavy tourism development in the late 2010s, the bay's setting of turquoise sea against swaying palms remains genuinely spectacular.

tourism_samples.title: Coconut Tree Hill, Mirissa
tourism_samples.description: Coconut Tree Hill is a small rocky headland at the eastern end of Mirissa Beach, topped by a photogenic cluster of tall coconut palms that lean dramatically over the sea and have become one of the most reproduced landscape images on the Sri Lankan south coast. The 10-minute climb from the beach rewards visitors with a panoramic view of Mirissa's full crescent bay, the fishing harbour, and on clear days the open Indian Ocean horizon. The spot is best visited at dawn before crowds arrive and is a popular photography target at both sunrise and sunset.

tourism_samples.title: Parrot Rock, Mirissa
tourism_samples.description: Parrot Rock is a wave-sculpted granite outcrop at the far eastern tip of Mirissa Beach, connected to the mainland by a sandy causeway that is passable on foot at low tide but surrounded by sea at high tide. A small sheltered pool on the landward side offers cautious bathing, while the ocean-facing side is battered by the full force of the Indian Ocean swell. The rock offers a unique vantage point directly above the whale-watching fleet as it leaves the harbour each morning during the November–April season.

tourism_samples.title: Polhena Beach
tourism_samples.description: Polhena is a wide, protected bay 2 km west of Matara town, where a natural reef barrier creates a large lagoon-like swimming zone that remains calm even when the open sea is rough—making it the most popular family swimming beach in the Matara district. The bay is lined with small cafés and beach umbrella stalls operated by the local community, and entry to the water is free and unrestricted. At the reef's edge, green and hawksbill turtles can be observed feeding on sea grass at very close range during any snorkelling visit.

tourism_samples.title: Polhena Reef
tourism_samples.description: The Polhena Reef is a fringing coral reef directly offshore from Polhena Beach near Matara, widely regarded as one of the most accessible turtle snorkelling sites in southern Sri Lanka. The reef hosts a resident population of green sea turtles that have become acclimatised to snorkeller presence, often feeding in sea-grass patches within arm's reach. Dive shops in Matara offer guided reef snorkelling and beginner scuba courses using the site, and snorkel equipment can be rented from vendors directly on the beach.

tourism_samples.title: Dutch Fort, Matara
tourism_samples.description: The Dutch Fort at Matara, constructed by the VOC around 1640 and expanded through the 17th century, is the best-preserved Dutch colonial fortification in Sri Lanka outside Galle, enclosing a section of the old town within curtain walls reinforced with corner bastions. Unlike the Galle UNESCO site, Matara Fort remains a functional urban neighbourhood with a colonial-era mosque (converted from a Dutch Reformed church), a clock tower, and residential streets within the walls. It is accessed by a single bridge across the Nilwala River and serves as the effective entry point to the old town.

tourism_samples.title: Matara Star Fort
tourism_samples.description: The Matara Star Fort is a unique pentagonal outer fortification built by the Dutch in 1763 across the Nilwala River from the main Matara Fort, intended to guard the river approach and the overland routes from the eastern districts. Its five bastion star-shaped plan is clearly visible from satellite imagery, and the structure is remarkably intact—the moat is partially preserved and the curtain walls stand to their original height. In a distinctly Sri Lankan repurposing of colonial military heritage, the fort's interior now houses the Matara Public Library.

tourism_samples.title: Dondra Head Lighthouse
tourism_samples.description: The Dondra Head Lighthouse marks the southernmost point of Sri Lanka, and at 49 metres it is the tallest lighthouse on the island, constructed by the British in 1890 from Cornish granite shipped from England and visible from 28 nautical miles at sea. The headland at Devi Nuwara (Dondra) was historically among the most sacred sites in the country—home to a great Buddhist temple razed by the Portuguese in 1587, of which only the inner sanctum survives. The cape marks where the Indian Ocean divides into the Bay of Bengal to the northeast and the Arabian Sea to the northwest.

tourism_samples.title: Gandara Beach
tourism_samples.description: Gandara is a narrow, wave-battered fishing beach on the exposed eastern flank of Matara town, separated from the calmer Polhena lagoon by the mouth of the Nilwala River. The beach is used almost exclusively by local fishermen who launch outrigger canoes directly through the shore break, and sees virtually no organised tourism despite its proximity to Matara's bus terminal and fort. The rivermouth environment—where freshwater meets ocean at a sand bar that shifts seasonally—creates a dynamic, photogenic landscape particularly at dawn.

tourism_samples.title: Medilla Beach
tourism_samples.description: Medilla is a small, rarely visited fishing beach a few kilometres east of Matara town in the direction of Dondra, where traditional outrigger canoes are hauled up on coarse sand backed by modest fishing homes. The beach has no tourist infrastructure, but the Dondra lighthouse headland is visible from the shore, lending a sense of being at the island's southernmost edge. Fishermen here use traditional beach-seine netting, a collective haul technique visible in the early mornings when the full team assembles on the sand.

tourism_samples.title: Dickwella Beach
tourism_samples.description: Dickwella is a low-key fishing town on the south coast roughly 15 km east of Matara, fronted by a broad sandy beach that sees very few foreign visitors despite its clean, open character and proximity to the Hummanaya blowhole at Kudawella. The beach faces southwest and receives moderate swells that have attracted a small community of local surfers, with an informal break forming over a sandy bar off the river mouth. The town serves as a practical base for exploring the quieter eastern stretch of the deep south coast.

tourism_samples.title: Hummanaya Blowhole, Kudawella
tourism_samples.description: The Hummanaya Blowhole near Kudawella village, approximately 3 km east of Dickwella, is one of the largest natural blowholes in Asia, where ocean swells are funnelled through a submarine volcanic rock channel and expelled as a geyser-like jet reaching up to 23 metres in height. The spectacle is most dramatic during the southwest monsoon between May and August, when large open-ocean swells drive water through the tunnel with tremendous force and the mist can be felt from the viewing platform above. A concrete staircase descends the cliff face to a safe observation terrace at close range.

tourism_samples.title: Hiriketiya Beach
tourism_samples.description: Hiriketiya is a deep horseshoe-shaped cove tucked beneath forested headlands a few kilometres west of Dikwella, discovered by the international surfing community in the mid-2010s and now a well-established destination on the south coast circuit. The bay generates a consistent beach break with a left and a right off a central sandbar, suitable for intermediate and advanced surfers, and the amphitheatre-like walls of tropical vegetation give the cove an intimate, enclosed atmosphere. The beach virtually disappears at high tide, leaving only a narrow strip of sand against the rocky base of the headlands.

tourism_samples.title: Talalla Beach
tourism_samples.description: Talalla is a remote, crescent beach approximately 20 km east of Matara, framed at both ends by rocky headlands and backed by sloping coconut plantations with no commercial development visible from the waterline. The beach is a documented sea turtle nesting site, and tidal rock pools at the eastern headland—rich in sea anemones, crabs, and small reef fish—attract snorkellers at low tide. The combination of physical isolation, natural shoreline, and reliable turtle activity makes it a favourite among travellers seeking authentic south-coast scenery.

tourism_samples.title: Mawella Beach
tourism_samples.description: Mawella is a tranquil, largely undiscovered beach west of Tangalle, where the eastern end borders a small lagoon and the shoreline is dotted with traditional outrigger fishing boats pulled up under coconut palms. The sea floor shelves gently and the water is clear during the northeast monsoon from October to March, making it suitable for casual swimming when more exposed beaches are rough. Mawella has appeared on 'hidden beaches' lists in recent years but retains a genuinely local character with minimal accommodation infrastructure.

tourism_samples.title: Rekawa Turtle Beach
tourism_samples.description: Rekawa, east of Tangalle, is one of the five most important sea turtle nesting beaches in Sri Lanka, monitored and protected by the Turtle Conservation Project since 1993 in a community-based programme that employs former poachers as guides. All five species that nest in Sri Lanka—leatherback, green, hawksbill, loggerhead, and olive ridley—have been recorded here, with leatherbacks most commonly encountered during deep-night watches. Community-guided observation sessions depart from the beach office after dark and operate under strict no-white-light and no-flash-photography regulations.

tourism_samples.title: Tangalle Beach
tourism_samples.description: Tangalle is a picturesque harbour town on the southeastern arc of the south coast, its main beach positioned beside a working fishing harbour where a colourful outrigger fleet returns at dawn each day with the overnight catch. The headland and bay system here creates several distinct beach segments—from the bustling town beach to the wild, undeveloped stretches east of the brackish lagoon—each with very different wave energy and atmosphere. The Buddhist vihara beside the beach that withstood the 2004 tsunami while the surrounding seafront was destroyed has become a quiet place of communal memory.

tourism_samples.title: Medaketiya Beach, Tangalle
tourism_samples.description: Medaketiya is a long, wild stretch of undeveloped shoreline extending east from Tangalle Bay, separated from the town by a brackish lagoon whose mouth migrates seasonally along the sand bar. The beach is backed by dense pandanus scrub and coastal dry-zone forest with virtually no permanent structures—one of the most pristine stretches of coast in the deep south and an active sea turtle nesting ground. Strong rip currents and a dumping shore break make swimming hazardous, but the beach is exceptional for solitary long walks and shorebird observation.

tourism_samples.title: Amanwella Beach, Tangalle
tourism_samples.description: The crescent of golden sand at Wellamulla, known as Amanwella Beach, lies on a secluded headland about 7 km east of Tangalle town and takes its name from the ultra-luxury Amanwella resort that occupies the hillside above it. The bay is sheltered by rocky promontories on both flanks, creating exceptionally calm, swimmable water in the inner arc while occasional surf-able conditions form at the edges during larger south swells. The surrounding dry-zone coconut estates and the absence of any road-side development give the beach a remote, frontier-like character fully unlike the busy western coast.

tourism_samples.title: Bentota Beach
tourism_samples.description: Bentota is Sri Lanka's most developed water-sports hub, positioned at the mouth of the Bentota River where a beach, broad lagoon, and estuarine river converge within a compact area to offer jet-skiing, windsurfing, water-skiing, wakeboarding, and banana boats all from a single stretch of sand. The wide sandy beach is backed by a row of international chain resort hotels that have operated here since the government developed the area in the 1970s as the country's first purpose-built beach resort zone. The adjacent Brief Garden—the idiosyncratic estate of landscape architect Bevis Bawa, brother of Geoffrey Bawa—is a frequent day-trip pairing.

tourism_samples.title: Induruwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Induruwa is a calm, mid-length beach between Bentota and Kosgoda on the western coast, characterised by a wide foreshore of pale sand backed by casuarina trees and a handful of mid-range hotels. The beach fronts the Induruwa Turtle Conservation Project, where nests collected from the shoreline are incubated in a protected hatchery and hatchlings released at dusk—one of a chain of such projects lining this stretch of coast. The sea is generally safe for swimming from November to April when the northeast monsoon keeps the western coast sheltered.

tourism_samples.title: Aluthgama Beach
tourism_samples.description: Aluthgama is a small riverside market town at the mouth of the Bentota River, where the estuary, lagoon, and open sea converge and boat-hire operators offer access to the Madu River mangrove system and the Brief Garden estate upstream. The beach south of the river mouth provides clean sand and moderate surf, while the riverbanks are lined with mangroves and boat-hire points for kayaks and canoes. The town's predominantly Muslim fish market—operating from 4 am—is one of the most vibrant on the western coast.

tourism_samples.title: Beruwala Beach and Lighthouse
tourism_samples.description: Beruwala is traditionally considered the site of the first Arab Muslim settlement in Sri Lanka, with Moorish traders establishing a community here around the 10th century CE—the Kechimalai Mosque on the Beruwala headland is among the oldest mosques in the country. The town's harbour is the largest fishing port on the western coast south of Colombo, and early-morning returns of the overnight fleet produce a spectacular scene of hundreds of boats unloading their catch on the jetty. The offshore Barberyn Rock Lighthouse—one of the oldest lighthouse structures on the Sri Lankan coast—is visible from the beach and accessible by boat.

tourism_samples.title: Moragalla Beach
tourism_samples.description: Moragalla is a low-key sandy beach between Beruwala and Aluthgama, set behind a coconut grove with a cluster of small family guesthouses and a largely local visitor character. The beach is calm and wide during the northeast monsoon season and is used primarily by guests of local accommodation and families from Kalutara, giving it a pleasantly uncrowded atmosphere compared to the resort zones immediately to the north. A short walk south leads to the Aluthgama fish market, worth visiting at dawn.

tourism_samples.title: Kalutara Beach
tourism_samples.description: Kalutara is a historic coastal town at the mouth of the Kalu Ganga—the most substantial river on the western coast—and the beach here is famed for the fine-grained cinnamon sand washed from the river's upper catchment in the wet zone. The Kalutara Bodhiya, a hollow dagoba containing a sacred Bo tree sapling, dominates the river crossing and is one of the most visited Buddhist pilgrimage sites in the Western Province, easily visible from the beach. Several large resort hotels line the long beachfront north and south of the river mouth.

tourism_samples.title: Wadduwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Wadduwa is a calm west-coast beach roughly halfway between Kalutara and Panadura, a popular weekend destination for Colombo residents thanks to its 45-minute drive from the city and a cluster of mid-range hotels along its palm-backed shore. The sea is generally safe for swimming from November through April, and the wide beach is used for beach volleyball, kite-flying, and sunset walks by local families. Wadduwa forms the northern section of a quietly pleasant coastal drive through Payagala and Beruwala.

tourism_samples.title: Payagala Beach
tourism_samples.description: Payagala is a wide, open beach between Kalutara and Beruwala where a small river outlet creates interesting tidal morphology and occasional freshwater pools at the top of the beach. The shoreline is popular on weekends with families from Colombo and Kalutara, served by beach vendors selling savoury snacks and king coconuts from carts on the sand. It remains significantly less developed than Beruwala to the south, with a predominantly local-use character and minimal tourist-facing infrastructure.

tourism_samples.title: Panadura Beach
tourism_samples.description: Panadura is an urban coastal town 26 km south of Colombo, historically notable for the Great Panadura Vadaya of 1873—a landmark public debate between Buddhist and Christian clergy that sparked a Buddhist revival movement with global repercussions, inspiring both Anagarika Dharmapala and Colonel Henry Steel Olcott. The Panadura Lagoon mouth creates a varied beach environment, and an open beach stretch south of the lagoon is used by local surfers accessing consistent beach-break waves. Excellent fresh seafood is available at the market near the lagoon mouth.

tourism_samples.title: Moratuwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Moratuwa is an industrialised coastal city south of Colombo, home to the University of Moratuwa and a major furniture manufacturing industry, but its beachfront at Rawathawatte provides an open stretch of ocean coast that functions as the primary beach for thousands of urban residents. The beach faces southwest and receives moderate swells in the monsoon season, making it unsuitable for casual swimming in those months but popular year-round for evening walks and kite-flying. The adjacent Lunawa Lagoon is a protected wetland managed for urban biodiversity.

tourism_samples.title: Mount Lavinia Beach
tourism_samples.description: Mount Lavinia Beach is the most celebrated urban beach in Sri Lanka, positioned 12 km south of Colombo Fort and dominated by the colonial-era Mount Lavinia Hotel—built as the official residence of British Governor Sir Edward Barnes in 1805 and now the country's most storied colonial hotel—whose wedding-cake facade overlooks the sea from a low bluff. The beach is a wide arc of brownish sand backed by a dense strip of seafood restaurants, beach bars, and surf-hire shops, busy every evening when Colombo residents come to swim and eat fresh fish grilled on the sand. A natural rocky headland at the hotel end creates a sheltered bay used for casual sea bathing and snorkelling.

tourism_samples.title: Galle Face Green, Colombo
tourism_samples.description: Galle Face Green is a 5-hectare oceanfront promenade in central Colombo stretching 500 metres from the colonial Galle Face Hotel to the edge of the Colombo Fort business district, originally laid out by the British in 1856 as a recreational esplanade. The green is the social heart of Colombo—packed each evening with families eating street food, flying kites, and watching the Indian Ocean sunset—and its seafront location makes it the city's most important public interface with the sea. The Colombo skyline, including the 356-metre Lotus Tower to the north, frames the seaward views in both directions along the promenade.

tourism_samples.title: Dehiwala Beach
tourism_samples.description: Dehiwala is an urban suburb just north of Mount Lavinia whose beachfront, though less frequented by tourists, is intensely used by local residents—particularly around the fish landing site where outrigger boats return each morning with the catch. The Dehiwala Zoological Gardens—Sri Lanka's national zoo, established in 1936—lies only a few hundred metres from the shoreline and is a major draw for Colombo-area families. The beach here has a dual character, transitioning between a working fishing foreshore in the mornings and a public recreation zone in the evenings.

tourism_samples.title: Negombo Beach
tourism_samples.description: Negombo is the principal beach resort north of Colombo, only 8 km from Bandaranaike International Airport and thus the de facto gateway beach for international arrivals, with dozens of hotels, restaurants, and dive shops catering to travellers starting and ending their Sri Lanka visits here. The city retains a strongly Catholic identity inherited from Portuguese missionaries who focused conversion efforts on the fishing community from the 16th century—evidenced by an extraordinary density of churches, roadside shrines, and annual saint's-day festivals. The beach itself is a long sandy strip backed by palm trees and beach bars, most enjoyable from November to March.

tourism_samples.title: Negombo Lagoon
tourism_samples.description: The Negombo Lagoon is a 9,000-hectare coastal lagoon separated from the sea by a narrow barrier beach and connected to the Dutch-built Hamilton Canal running south to Colombo—together forming a historic inland waterway. The lagoon is one of the most productive fisheries in Sri Lanka, supporting thousands of families in the communities on its shores through cast-net fishing, basket trapping, and aquaculture of prawns and fish in pens. Early-morning boat tours from the Negombo fish market navigate channels lined with mangroves, stilted fishing platforms, and feeding flocks of egrets and herons.

tourism_samples.title: Hamilton Canal (Dutch Canal), Negombo
tourism_samples.description: The Hamilton Canal is a 120 km inland waterway connecting Negombo to Colombo, built by the Dutch East India Company in the 18th century to transport cinnamon—Sri Lanka's most valuable export commodity—safely overland away from coastal piracy and monsoon weather. The canal threads through mangrove channels, paddy fields, and urban corridors, and portions between Negombo and Ja-Ela are navigable by kayak and used for early-morning birdwatching tours targeting kingfishers, purple herons, and estuarine crocodiles. The canal's tow paths and bridges are part of the cycling circuit around the Negombo Lagoon.

tourism_samples.title: Lellama Fishing Harbour, Negombo
tourism_samples.description: Lellama is the main fishing harbour of Negombo and one of the busiest and most visually dramatic fish markets in Sri Lanka, where the entire west-coast overnight catch is landed, auctioned, and dispatched to Colombo's restaurants and supermarkets before dawn each morning. The harbour is tightly packed with brightly painted outrigger trawlers, and market stalls offer a staggering diversity of Sri Lankan seafood—Spanish mackerel, swordfish, tiger prawns, squid, and crab—at prices far below Colombo retail. Guided pre-dawn fish market tours depart from Negombo town hotels at around 5 am.

tourism_samples.title: Pamunugama Beach
tourism_samples.description: Pamunugama is a traditional Catholic fishing village on the northern outskirts of Negombo where the beach retains the authentic character of a working shore-based fishery—boats are built and repaired on the sand, nets are dried on bamboo racks, and the community is almost entirely engaged in the sea. The village's large whitewashed church hosts an annual feast in August that draws thousands of pilgrims from across the west coast. The beach stretches north towards the Negombo Lagoon mouth and is largely undeveloped compared to the main Negombo tourist strip.

tourism_samples.title: Waikkal Beach
tourism_samples.description: Waikkal is a peaceful beachfront village roughly 15 km north of Negombo, where the Daduru Oya river meets the sea in a small estuary that serves as a boat-building yard and artisanal fishing base. The beach is broad, clean, and almost entirely absent of tourist infrastructure—a handful of eco-guesthouses cater to birdwatchers and canoe enthusiasts exploring the mangrove-lined river channels. The estuary is documented habitat for estuarine crocodiles, and dawn paddles upstream can yield sightings of monitor lizards, painted storks, and sea eagles.

tourism_samples.title: Marawila Beach
tourism_samples.description: Marawila is a quiet mid-west coast beach town between Chilaw and Negombo, characterised by a long straight beach backed by dense coconut plantations and a handful of unassuming hotels offering some of the lowest beachside accommodation prices in Sri Lanka. The beach provides reliable swimming conditions during the northeast monsoon dry season (November–April) and is used for beach cricket and kite-flying by local families on weekends. The inland villages around Marawila are known for hand-printed batik production, easily combined with a beach visit.

tourism_samples.title: Chilaw Beach and Lagoon
tourism_samples.description: Chilaw is a predominantly Catholic fishing town on the northwest coast built around a large coastal lagoon, a habitat supporting extensive commercial fisheries and mangrove ecosystems that are explored by boat from the town landing. The wild ocean beach at the lagoon mouth is wave-swept and used exclusively for artisanal fishing, while the inner lagoon is calm and productive. The sacred Munnesvaram Hindu temple complex inland from Chilaw—one of the five ishwarams of Sri Lanka—attracts pilgrims from across the island during its annual perahera festival in August.

tourism_samples.title: Wennapuwa Beach
tourism_samples.description: Wennapuwa is a small Catholic fishing settlement between Chilaw and Negombo, where the beach is lined with large Seine nets dried on bamboo racks and traditional outrigger trawlers maintained by the village's fishing families. The nearby St. Anne's Church at Thalawila—a short drive north along the coast—is among the most sacred Catholic shrines in Sri Lanka and draws hundreds of thousands of pilgrims each July, turning the coastal road into one of the busiest pilgrimage routes on the island. The beach itself is undeveloped and accessible but has no visitor facilities.

tourism_samples.title: Kalpitiya Beach
tourism_samples.description: Kalpitiya, on a narrow peninsula north of Puttalam, is Sri Lanka's kite-surfing capital, where the interaction between the sea breeze and the wide, shallow Puttalam Lagoon generates consistent thermal winds from May to October ideal for kiteboarding and windsurfing. The peninsula's ocean-facing beaches are also exceptional for dolphin encounters—spinner dolphins are observed in pods of hundreds off the northern tip year-round—and blue whales pass the area on their seasonal migration. Eco-lodges and kite-surfing centres have established themselves here among the traditionally Muslim fishing communities of the peninsula.

tourism_samples.title: Alankuda Beach, Kalpitiya
tourism_samples.description: Alankuda is an isolated strip of pristine white sand on the lagoon-facing eastern side of the Kalpitiya Peninsula, sheltered from ocean swell and offering dramatically calm turquoise waters contrasting with the wild ocean beaches on the western face. The beach is the base for Sri Lanka's most-cited dolphin-watching operations—large pods of spinner dolphins are regularly encountered in the lagoon channels within 15 minutes of departure from the beach. Several upmarket eco-camps and boutique kite-surfing lodges have developed here with low environmental footprints.

tourism_samples.title: Bar Reef Marine Sanctuary, Kalpitiya
tourism_samples.description: Bar Reef, approximately 16 km offshore from the Kalpitiya Peninsula, is the largest coral reef system in Sri Lanka and one of the most biologically significant marine ecosystems in the Indian Ocean, designated as a Marine Sanctuary in 1992 and covering over 300 km². The reef complex supports more than 100 coral species, 120 fish species, six ray species, and regular aggregations of whale sharks, spinner dolphins, and bottlenose dolphins. Liveaboard diving expeditions from Kalpitiya operate during the northeast monsoon season (November–March) when sea conditions allow transit to the outer reef.

tourism_samples.title: Dutch Fort, Kalpitiya
tourism_samples.description: The Kalpitiya Fort was originally built by the Portuguese in 1667 and captured and rebuilt by the Dutch in 1672 as a strategic garrison controlling the entrance to Puttalam Lagoon and the pearl fishery grounds of the Gulf of Mannar. The fort is one of the very few Dutch colonial military structures on Sri Lanka's northwest coast, and its thick laterite walls and corner bastions are largely intact. A portion of the fort on the ocean-facing side is accessible to visitors, though the interior is now operated as a Sri Lanka Navy facility.

tourism_samples.title: Puttalam Lagoon
tourism_samples.description: Puttalam Lagoon is the largest coastal lagoon in Sri Lanka at approximately 21,000 hectares, separated from the Indian Ocean by the Kalpitiya Peninsula in an extraordinarily rich brackish ecosystem of mangroves, sea-grass beds, and open water. The lagoon supports commercial prawn and fish farming alongside traditional canoe-based fisheries and hosts seasonal flamingo populations that migrate from northwest India between November and April. Boat excursions from Kalpitiya or Puttalam town navigate narrow mangrove channels past pearl oyster cultivation rafts, egret colonies, and traditional fish-trap lines.

tourism_samples.title: Silawatura Beach, Kalpitiya
tourism_samples.description: Silawatura is a remote settlement on the northwestern tip of the Kalpitiya Peninsula, fronted by an isolated beach of fine white sand facing the open Gulf of Mannar, accessible only by a long drive along a sandy peninsula track or by boat from Kalpitiya town. The beach is practically unspoiled—the only infrastructure is a small fishing hamlet and a small lighthouse—and loggerhead turtles nest here between October and February with minimal human disturbance. The sunsets from Silawatura over the open sea are considered among the most dramatic on the entire northwestern coast.

tourism_samples.title: Pomparippu Beach
tourism_samples.description: Pomparippu is a historically significant site on Sri Lanka's remote northwest coast north of Wilpattu, where Archaeological Department excavations uncovered an extensive protohistoric burial ground—urn-burials and skeletal remains dated to approximately the 5th–6th centuries BCE—ranking it among the most important Iron Age sites in Sri Lanka. The beach is wide and windswept, abutting the northern boundary of Wilpattu National Park, and sees almost no tourism. Tracks through coastal scrub lead to the park boundary where elephants, leopards, and sloth bears range to within sight of the sea.

tourism_samples.title: Crow Island Beach Park, Colombo
tourism_samples.description: Crow Island Beach Park is a small public recreation beach on the northern fringe of Colombo, occupying a narrow spit at the Kelani River estuary mouth just south of the Ja-Ela bridge. The park is surrounded by the Colombo Port industrial zone and the estuary, giving it a dramatic urban-industrial-maritime backdrop unusual for a Sri Lankan beach park. It is primarily used by Colombo's northern suburban communities and school groups, and offers unobstructed views of large container ships entering and leaving the capital's port.

tourism_samples.title: Bangadeniya Beach
tourism_samples.description: Bangadeniya is a quiet stretch of northwest coast beach between Chilaw and Nattandiya, where a small river and estuary create a varied coastal landscape of sand, mangrove fringe, and open sea. The beach is used exclusively by local fishing communities and occasional domestic visitors, with no formal tourist infrastructure, making it a rare example of an undisturbed coastal community beach within a two-hour drive of Colombo. The area forms part of the broader cultural circuit of the Munnesvaram temple on the northwest coast.

tourism_samples.title: Arugam Bay Main Point
tourism_samples.description: Arugam Bay Main Point is one of the most celebrated right-hand point breaks in Asia, consistently ranked among the world's top surf destinations and attracting professional and amateur surfers from across the globe during the May–October surfing season. The wave breaks over a sand and reef bottom off the southern headland of Arugam Bay, producing rides of up to 300 metres on a good south swell, with distinct sections ranging from a steep take-off to a long, rolling shoulder. The village of Pottuvil behind the bay has developed a well-rounded surf tourism infrastructure of guesthouses, yoga studios, seafood shacks, and surf-hire shops.

tourism_samples.title: Whiskey Point, Arugam Bay
tourism_samples.description: Whiskey Point is a right-hand reef and beach break approximately 4 km north of Arugam Bay village, accessed by a short tuk-tuk ride along the coast road and offering a less crowded alternative to the main point during peak season. The wave breaks over a shallow reef and can produce hollow, punchy barrels on bigger swells, making it popular with intermediate and advanced surfers seeking performance conditions. A handful of simple camps and bungalows have established themselves around the headland, attracting surfers who prefer a quieter atmosphere.

tourism_samples.title: Pottuvil Point Surf Break
tourism_samples.description: Pottuvil Point is a left-hand surf break at the northern end of the Arugam Bay system, breaking at the rivermouth where the Heda Oya meets the sea, and is one of the few left-handers in an area dominated by right-hand point breaks. The wave is less consistent than Main Point but provides long walls on south swells and sees significantly fewer surfers, appealing to those seeking uncrowded conditions. The river mouth adjacent to the break creates a dynamic estuarine environment frequented by wading birds and occasionally estuarine crocodiles.

tourism_samples.title: Peanut Farm Surf Break, Arugam Bay
tourism_samples.description: Peanut Farm is a right-hand beach break approximately 5 km south of Arugam Bay Main Point, accessed by a dirt track through a groundnut-farming area that gives the break its name. The wave is less structured than the main point but can produce excellent, uncrowded conditions during larger south swells, and is a favourite second session spot for surfers staying in the Bay. The remote access and absence of commercial infrastructure give the break a wild, backcountry character rarely encountered on the main tourist circuit.

tourism_samples.title: Elephant Rock Surf Break, Arugam Bay
tourism_samples.description: Elephant Rock is a surf spot south of Arugam Bay named for a large boulder formation on the headland, producing a right-hand wave over a shallow reef that breaks with more power and steepness than the main point on similar sized swells. The surrounding landscape is wild and sparsely inhabited, with the rock formation itself forming a dramatic photographic backdrop to the surf. The break is best accessed by motorbike or tuk-tuk along the sand track south of Pottuvil.

tourism_samples.title: Arugam Bay Lagoon
tourism_samples.description: The Arugam Bay Lagoon stretches behind the main beach, separated from the ocean by a narrow sand bar and supporting a rich brackish ecosystem of mangroves, reed beds, and open water. The lagoon is an important waterbird habitat—flamingos, painted storks, grey herons, and purple herons are regularly observed from the bank—and estuarine crocodiles are present in the mangrove channels, most often seen from a canoe or kayak at dusk. Guided paddling tours of the lagoon depart from the village and offer a compelling wildlife counterpoint to the surfing and beach activities on the ocean side.

tourism_samples.title: Panama Beach
tourism_samples.description: Panama Beach is a remote, undeveloped stretch of coast south of Arugam Bay near the small agricultural village of Panama, one of the few beaches on the east coast that remains entirely free of tourist infrastructure and where sea turtles nest without disturbance from development. The beach is accessed via a dirt track through dry-zone scrub forest and lagoon margins, and the drive itself passes through landscapes typical of the transition zone between the cultural heartland and the Kumana wilderness. The beach marks the beginning of the Kumana National Park coastal corridor.

tourism_samples.title: Okanda Beach and Kovil
tourism_samples.description: Okanda sits at the southern gate of Kumana National Park, where a small beach fronts a rocky headland topped by the Okanda Murugan Kovil—a sacred Hindu shrine that forms the southern terminus of the annual Pada Yatra pilgrimage during which tens of thousands of devotees walk along the coast from Jaffna each July. The beach here is exposed and wave-swept, backed by coastal scrub where leopards, elephants, and sloth bears pass through regularly in proximity to the pilgrimage route. The kovil's position directly above the sea, with waves breaking on the rocks below, makes it one of the most dramatically sited Hindu shrines in Sri Lanka.

tourism_samples.title: Kumana Beach, Kumana National Park
tourism_samples.description: The coastal strip within Kumana National Park south of Okanda is an entirely protected beach accessible only on a licensed park safari, running through dry-zone forest where elephants and water buffalo routinely emerge from the scrub onto the sand. The beach forms part of one of Sri Lanka's most important coastal bird habitats—the Kumana Villu wetland complex inland supports the largest breeding colony of painted storks in Sri Lanka alongside spoonbills, open-bill storks, and herons. Sea turtle nesting is undisturbed here by human activity, making the Kumana coast one of the most pristine nesting environments on the east coast.

tourism_samples.title: Pasikuda Beach
tourism_samples.description: Pasikuda Beach on the mid-east coast is one of the most distinctive beaches in Sri Lanka, renowned for an exceptionally shallow, flat bay where the water remains barely waist-deep for over 300 metres from the shoreline—ideal for children and non-swimmers. The crystal-clear, calm turquoise water and white-sand bottom make Pasikuda one of the most photographed beaches in the country, and the Sri Lankan government has invested substantially in resort development here following the end of the civil war in 2009. The beach and the adjacent Kalkudah bay form a continuous arc of protected, reef-sheltered water stretching several kilometres.

tourism_samples.title: Kalkudah Beach
tourism_samples.description: Kalkudah Beach is a pristine, curving bay immediately north of Pasikuda, sharing the same extraordinary shallow reef-protected calm but receiving significantly fewer visitors due to less developed tourism infrastructure. The beach was a popular resort destination in the 1970s before the civil war effectively closed the east coast to tourism for nearly three decades, and the Dutch Bay Hotel ruins visible in the vegetation are a reminder of its pre-war history. Kalkudah is now recovering as a destination and offers a quieter, more spacious alternative to the increasingly developed Pasikuda beach.

tourism_samples.title: Batticaloa Lagoon
tourism_samples.description: The Batticaloa Lagoon is a 72 km-long coastal lagoon stretching between Batticaloa town and the sea, forming the dominant geographical feature of the Batticaloa District and supporting an extensive fishery, a pearl oyster aquaculture industry, and the Dutch-built fort at its narrowest point. The lagoon is the site of the famous 'singing fish' phenomenon—an unexplained harmonic humming audible from a boat on still nights near the Kallady Bridge, attributed variously to bivalve molluscs, wind, or acoustic effects of the water column. The lagoon is navigable by boat for its full length and provides a remarkable journey through mangrove channels, fishing villages, and Hindu temple islets.

tourism_samples.title: Batticaloa Fort
tourism_samples.description: Batticaloa Fort is a Dutch colonial fortification built in 1665 on a small island within the Batticaloa Lagoon, replacing an earlier Portuguese structure and occupying a strategically commanding position where two lagoon channels meet. The fort is in a reasonable state of preservation and now houses government offices, a small museum, and a lighthouse that continues to function as a navigational aid. The unusual island setting, reached by the Kallady Bridge, gives the fort a picturesque, moat-like character entirely different from the ocean-front forts of the south and northwest coasts.

tourism_samples.title: Kallady Beach, Batticaloa
tourism_samples.description: Kallady is a beachfront neighbourhood on the seaward side of Batticaloa, positioned at the eastern end of the historic Lady Manning Bridge—one of the longest causeways crossing the Batticaloa Lagoon—where the lagoon narrows before meeting the Indian Ocean. The beach here faces open sea and receives substantial swell from the Bay of Bengal during the northeast monsoon, making it unsuitable for swimming but atmospheric and dramatic. The bridge itself is the traditional observation point for the mysterious 'singing fish' acoustic phenomenon unique to the Batticaloa Lagoon.

tourism_samples.title: Vakarai Beach
tourism_samples.description: Vakarai is a coastal village on the northern shore of Batticaloa District, where a long, largely deserted beach backed by dry-zone forest meets the Indian Ocean in a landscape that saw significant conflict during the civil war but is now a tranquil, recovering area of natural beauty. The offshore waters are a productive zone for whale watching—blue whales and sperm whales are reported seasonally—and the beach itself has rare undisturbed sea-turtle nesting sites. A government-run ecotourism initiative has begun developing the area carefully as a post-war community tourism destination.

tourism_samples.title: Uppuveli Beach
tourism_samples.description: Uppuveli is a calm, palm-backed beach 4 km north of Trincomalee town, functioning as the closest accessible resort beach to the city and offering clean swimming conditions in its sheltered bay from February to September before the northeast monsoon breaks. The beach is lined with a selection of mid-range guesthouses and seafood restaurants and is frequented by independent travellers using it as a base for day trips to Pigeon Island and Fort Frederick. A Commonwealth War Cemetery near the beach contains the graves of over 360 Allied naval personnel killed during the Pacific Theatre battles of 1942–1944.

tourism_samples.title: Nilaveli Beach
tourism_samples.description: Nilaveli is a wide, straight stretch of pristine white sand running several kilometres north of Uppuveli, regarded as one of the finest beaches in the country for its powder-soft sand, clear turquoise water, and backdrop of coconut palms. The beach is the departure point for snorkelling and diving excursions to Pigeon Island National Park approximately 1 km offshore, and the clarity of the near-shore water at Nilaveli itself is exceptional for swimming and casual snorkelling. The beach was largely closed to tourism for decades during the civil war and its relative underdevelopment gives it a spaciousness difficult to find on the more commercialised south and west coasts.

tourism_samples.title: Pigeon Island National Park
tourism_samples.description: Pigeon Island National Park lies approximately 1 km offshore from Nilaveli Beach and encompasses two small rocky islands and their surrounding coral reef, designated as a marine national park in 2003 primarily to protect Sri Lanka's last remaining reef of hard corals in the Northern Province. The reef supports populations of blacktip reef sharks, hawksbill turtles, reef octopus, and over 100 species of coral fish including large Napoleon wrasse and barracuda schools. Day-trip snorkelling excursions depart from Nilaveli and Uppuveli beaches on fibreglass boats, with park entry fees paid directly to rangers on the island.

tourism_samples.title: Fort Frederick, Trincomalee
tourism_samples.description: Fort Frederick is a historic fortification occupying the eastern headland of Trincomalee harbour, originally constructed by the Portuguese in 1623, substantially rebuilt by the Dutch in the 1660s, and subsequently occupied by the British who held it until Sri Lankan independence in 1948. The fort contains significant historical structures including an inscription rock, a British-era clock tower, and the gateway through which civilians walk daily to access the Koneswaram Hindu temple on Swami Rock at the far end of the peninsula. The Sri Lanka Army currently administers the fort, and a section including the temple approach is open to the public.

tourism_samples.title: Swami Rock and Koneswaram Kovil, Trincomalee
tourism_samples.description: Swami Rock is a dramatic 130-metre cliff at the tip of the Trincomalee headland on which the Koneswaram Kovil—one of the five ancient Pancha Ishwaram Shiva temples of Sri Lanka—stands at the very edge of the precipice above the Indian Ocean. The site was known to ancient Tamil and Sinhalese chronicles and to Arab, Chinese, and European navigators, and the original temple was reportedly thrown into the sea by Portuguese forces in 1624 before being rebuilt in 1963. The headland is also called Lover's Leap, a name from a colonial-era legend, and the view from the cliff edge over the vast blue expanse of the harbour and open ocean is considered one of the most spectacular in eastern Sri Lanka.

tourism_samples.title: Marble Beach, Trincomalee
tourism_samples.description: Marble Beach is a sheltered, crystal-clear cove about 5 km north of Trincomalee town along the inner harbour road, historically maintained as an exclusive recreational beach for the British Royal Navy and later the Sri Lanka Navy, now open to the public. The beach gets its name from its distinctively pale, fine-grained sand and the remarkable clarity of the water in its enclosed bay—it is consistently cited as the cleanest and most pristine beach in the Trincomalee district. The surrounding hills and the absence of development around the bay maintain the protected, private atmosphere of its naval heritage.

tourism_samples.title: Dutch Bay Beach, Trincomalee
tourism_samples.description: Dutch Bay is a sheltered bay on the western side of the Trincomalee peninsula facing the harbour, flanked by the Dutch colonial fortifications and the modern town, and serving as the main accessible waterfront for Trincomalee residents. The bay was named by Dutch cartographers who first surveyed the harbour in the 17th century and provides calm, sheltered swimming conditions in contrast to the open-ocean Uppuveli beach to the north. The waterfront here gives directly onto the main town beach promenade where seafood stalls and fishing boats create the characteristic harbour-edge atmosphere of the town.

tourism_samples.title: Foul Point Lighthouse, Trincomalee
tourism_samples.description: Foul Point, at the southern entrance to Trincomalee harbour, is marked by one of the oldest lighthouses on the east coast of Sri Lanka, a British-constructed structure guiding ships through the harbour approach since the colonial era. The point commands a panoramic view across the harbour entrance toward Mutur on the opposite shore and was a strategically critical naval observation post during World War II when the Japanese fleet attacked Trincomalee in April 1942. The road to the lighthouse passes through a wild stretch of coastal scrub inhabited by monitor lizards and nesting seabirds.

tourism_samples.title: Great Sober Island, Trincomalee
tourism_samples.description: Great Sober Island is a small, uninhabited island in the outer reaches of Trincomalee harbour, used for day-trip snorkelling and picnicking excursions from Uppuveli and Nilaveli beaches due to its fringing reef of live coral and its clean, accessible shore. The island's name appears on Royal Navy charts from the 18th century and the surrounding waters were the site of the 1942 Battle of Ceylon, during which British carrier aircraft engaged Japanese naval forces. The reef around the island supports sea turtles, reef fish, and—on calm days—spinner dolphin pods that enter the harbour.

tourism_samples.title: Kuchaveli Beach
tourism_samples.description: Kuchaveli (also spelled Kuchchaveli) is a remote, pristine beach approximately 30 km north of Trincomalee, where a long arc of untouched white sand backed by dry coastal forest meets the Indian Ocean in a setting almost entirely free of tourism development. The beach is one of the few east-coast locations where the combination of isolation, water clarity, and unspoiled natural landscape makes it comparable to remote Maldivian islands. A small number of eco-guesthouses have begun operating in the area, but the majority of the beach remains accessible only to travellers willing to hire a tuk-tuk or motorcycle from Trincomalee.

tourism_samples.title: Pulmoddai Beach
tourism_samples.description: Pulmoddai is a coastal village north of Trincomalee notable as the site of Sri Lanka's largest mineral sand extraction operation, where ilmenite, rutile, and zircon—heavy mineral sands deposited by ancient river and coastal processes—are commercially mined from the beach. Despite the industrial activity, the surrounding coastline is wide and undeveloped, and the village's beach-side location at the mouth of the Nay Aru lagoon creates a varied estuary environment. The beach is rarely visited by tourists but the juxtaposition of a working mineral beach and pristine lagoon is ecologically and industrially fascinating.

tourism_samples.title: Thiriyaya Beach
tourism_samples.description: Thiriyaya is an isolated coastal site about 80 km north of Trincomalee in Trincomalee District, most notable for a small rocky headland above the beach on which a remarkably well-preserved ancient Buddhist stupa stands—believed by some local tradition to date from the reign of Emperor Ashoka's missionary son Mihindu, though archaeological consensus places it in the early Anuradhapura period. The beach below the stupa is wave-swept and deserted, accessible only via a rough coastal track, and the combination of ancient sacred ruin and wild Indian Ocean scenery is extraordinarily evocative. The site is almost entirely unknown to foreign visitors and sees only occasional pilgrimages by local Buddhists.

tourism_samples.title: Trincomalee Harbour
tourism_samples.description: Trincomalee Harbour is one of the finest natural deep-water harbours in the world, a vast enclosed bay roughly 13 km long and 6 km wide capable of berthing the largest naval vessels afloat—a quality that made it strategically coveted by the Portuguese, Dutch, French, and British in succession. The harbour was the base of the British East Indies Fleet during World War II and the site of the April 1942 Japanese carrier air attack on British naval forces in one of the most significant naval actions of the Pacific War. Today the harbour supports the Sri Lanka Navy's Eastern Command, a commercial port, and an emerging diving and whale-watching tourism sector centred on the surrounding reefs and offshore islands.

tourism_samples.title: Crocodile Rock, Arugam Bay
tourism_samples.description: Crocodile Rock is a distinctive wave-sculpted boulder formation on a headland immediately south of Arugam Bay village, framing a right-hand reef break that produces consistent, user-friendly waves on smaller south swells and is considered one of the more forgiving performance breaks in the Arugam Bay system. The rock formation itself—elongated and low-lying, resembling a basking reptile from a distance—is a well-known local landmark and the break is clearly visible from the main beach promenade. The spot is often used as an afternoon option when the main point is crowded, and the adjacent rocky platform provides a natural grandstand for watching surfers.

tourism_samples.title: Pidurutalagala Peak
tourism_samples.description: At 2,524 metres, Pidurutalagala is the highest mountain in Sri Lanka, rising immediately north of Nuwara Eliya town and crowned by a government telecommunications and military installation that restricts public access to the summit but not to the approach trails. The mountain's upper slopes are blanketed in montane cloud forest harbouring a remarkable density of endemic plant species, including several orchid varieties found nowhere else on earth. The approach trail from Nuwara Eliya through the pine plantations offers progressively expanding views over the surrounding tea-covered highlands before the restricted zone begins.

tourism_samples.title: Gregory Lake, Nuwara Eliya
tourism_samples.description: Gregory Lake is an artificial reservoir created in 1873 by British Governor Sir William Henry Gregory by damming the Nanu Oya river on the western fringe of Nuwara Eliya, and today forms the centrepiece of the town's leisure activities. Pedal boats, motor boats, and horse rides along the lakeside bridle path are available throughout the day, and the early-morning mist that rolls across the water surface—framed by pine-forested hills—is one of the most recognisable images of the Sri Lankan hill country. The lake is encircled by a 5 km jogging track widely used by residents and visitors of the town.

tourism_samples.title: Victoria Park, Nuwara Eliya
tourism_samples.description: Victoria Park is a manicured public garden at the heart of Nuwara Eliya town, established during the British colonial era and named for Queen Victoria, featuring sweeping lawns, seasonal flowerbeds of antirrhinum, salvia, and roses, and a pavilion bandstand evocative of an English municipal park. The park is a significant birdwatching site in Sri Lanka, particularly during the northeast monsoon season from October to January when Indian pitta, Kashmir flycatcher, and pied thrush migrants join the resident endemic hill country species. A children's play area and a miniature stream running through the grounds make it a favourite gathering place for local families.

tourism_samples.title: Hakgala Botanical Garden
tourism_samples.description: Hakgala Botanical Garden is a 27-hectare highland garden established in 1861 at 1,745 metres elevation on the rocky slopes of the Hakgala massif, approximately 10 km southeast of Nuwara Eliya—originally planted to cultivate cinchona (quinine) bark and later converted to a botanical research station. The garden is divided into themed sections including a Fernery with over 100 fern species, a Rose Garden, a Japonicum rhododendron collection, and a rock garden embedded into the natural escarpment. According to Ramayana tradition, this forested rocky outcrop is one of the sites where Sita was held captive by Ravana, and the garden adjoins a forested wildlife sanctuary.

tourism_samples.title: Seetha Amman Temple, Nuwara Eliya
tourism_samples.description: The Seetha Amman Temple at Seetha Eliya, 5 km from Nuwara Eliya on the Badulla road, is a colourful Hindu shrine built directly beside the Seetha stream and occupying a site that forms one of the most visited stops on Sri Lanka's Ramayana pilgrimage circuit. The temple marks the location where, according to the epic, the demon king Ravana held the princess Sita captive in the Ashoka Vatika garden, and curious circular impressions in the streambed rocks are pointed out by the priests as imprints left by Ravana's chariot. The site draws Tamil Hindu pilgrims from across Sri Lanka and the Indian diaspora, particularly during the Navaratri festival.

tourism_samples.title: Nuwara Eliya Hill Station
tourism_samples.description: Nuwara Eliya is Sri Lanka's pre-eminent colonial hill station, established at 1,889 metres elevation by the British in the 1820s as a sanatorium and recreational retreat whose architecture—Tudor-gabled guest houses, a century-old golf course, a hill club with a dress code, and a horse racing track—has earned it the nickname 'Little England.'
